title: UN chooses New York City for its headquarters
blurb: The United Nations General Assembly voted to site its permanent headquarters in Manhattan after John D. Rockefeller Jr. donated $8.5 million to buy the East River tract.
detail: The 18-acre site along the East River between 42nd and 48th streets had been slaughterhouses and tenements. Rockefeller's gift broke a deadlock over whether the UN would settle in New York, Philadelphia or San Francisco. The choice cemented New York's postwar role as a global capital of diplomacy.

title: Levittown and the postwar suburban exodus
blurb: Construction began on Levittown on Long Island, the mass-produced suburb that helped pull white middle-class families out of the city.
detail: Abraham Levitt and his sons built more than 17,000 nearly identical homes using assembly-line methods, offering GIs affordable ownership. Federally backed mortgages steered investment to the suburbs while redlining starved city neighborhoods. Levittown's covenants barred Black buyers, seeding decades of regional segregation.

title: Jackie Robinson breaks baseball's color barrier
blurb: Jackie Robinson debuted at first base for the Brooklyn Dodgers, becoming the first Black player in the modern major leagues.
detail: Robinson took the field at Ebbets Field against the Boston Braves after Dodgers president Branch Rickey signed him. He endured torrents of abuse yet won Rookie of the Year and helped integrate the national pastime. His No. 42 is now retired across all of Major League Baseball.

title: Idlewild Airport opens in Queens
blurb: New York International Airport, universally known as Idlewild, opened in Queens and was dedicated by President Truman that summer.
detail: Built on the marshy site of the Idlewild golf course, it relieved the crowded LaGuardia Field and became the nation's premier gateway for international travel. It was renamed John F. Kennedy International Airport in 1963 after the president's assassination. Its jet-age TWA Flight Center later became an architectural landmark.

title: UN Secretariat tower opens on the East River
blurb: The glass-and-steel Secretariat Building, designed by an international team including Le Corbusier and Oscar Niemeyer, was occupied by UN staff through 1950 and 1951.
detail: The 39-story slab, wrapped in green glass curtain walls, became an icon of postwar modernism on the Manhattan skyline. Wallace Harrison led the design board that shaped the full complex, including the General Assembly hall completed in 1952. The headquarters is international territory not subject to New York City zoning.

title: The Puerto Rican Great Migration peaks
blurb: Puerto Rican migration to New York City hit its high point in the early 1950s, remaking neighborhoods from East Harlem to the South Bronx.
detail: Cheap air travel from San Juan and mainland labor demand drew hundreds of thousands of migrants, US citizens since 1917. By 1960 more than 600,000 Puerto Ricans lived in the city, building El Barrio and Loisaida. The influx reshaped city politics, music and Spanish-language culture for generations.

title: The Brooklyn Dodgers win their only World Series
blurb: The Dodgers beat the Yankees in Game 7 to claim Brooklyn's first and only World Series championship.
detail: Johnny Podres pitched a shutout and Sandy Amoros made a famous catch to seal the 2-0 win at Yankee Stadium. After decades of near-misses summed up in the cry 'wait till next year,' the borough finally celebrated. The triumph became bittersweet when the team decamped west just two years later.

title: The Dodgers and Giants leave for California
blurb: Owner Walter O'Malley moved the Dodgers to Los Angeles while the New York Giants left for San Francisco, ending an era of National League baseball in the city.
detail: O'Malley failed to secure a new Brooklyn stadium and chased the untapped West Coast market. Ebbets Field was demolished in 1960 and replaced by public housing. The dual departure left the Yankees alone until the Mets debuted in 1962 to fill the void.

title: Containerization and the decline of the port
blurb: The rise of container shipping shifted cargo from Manhattan and Brooklyn's finger piers to modern terminals in New Jersey, hollowing out the historic waterfront.
detail: Malcom McLean's containers, first shipped from Newark in 1956, needed vast flat backlands the old city docks lacked. Port Newark and later Elizabeth captured the trade, and tens of thousands of longshore jobs vanished. The Brooklyn and West Side piers fell silent, reshaping the city's economy and shoreline.

sources.name: Smithsonian, National Museum of American History

title: The Cross Bronx Expressway is completed
blurb: Robert Moses's Cross Bronx Expressway was fully completed in 1963 after more than a decade of construction that displaced thousands and cut the borough in two.
detail: The seven-mile highway plowed through dense neighborhoods like East Tremont, evicting an estimated 5,000 families. Championed by Moses over fierce resistance, it became a symbol of top-down urban renewal's human cost. Many historians link it to the South Bronx's later collapse into blight and arson.

title: Demolition of the original Penn Station begins
blurb: Wreckers started tearing down McKim, Mead & White's monumental Pennsylvania Station, an act that galvanized the American preservation movement.
detail: The 1910 Beaux-Arts masterpiece, modeled on the Baths of Caracalla, was razed over three years to build Madison Square Garden and an office tower. Its granite eagles and columns were dumped in the New Jersey Meadowlands. Critic Vincent Scully wrote that travelers once entered the city like gods and now scuttle in like rats.

title: The World's Fair opens in Flushing Meadows
blurb: The 1964-65 New York World's Fair opened in Queens under the theme 'Peace Through Understanding,' drawing more than 50 million visitors over two seasons.
detail: Robert Moses built the fair on the ashes of a former ash dump he had earlier turned into parkland. The 12-story stainless-steel Unisphere became its enduring emblem, and pavilions previewed technologies from touch-tone phones to Disney's animatronics. The fair lost money but left Flushing Meadows-Corona Park and its landmark structures behind.

title: The Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge opens
blurb: The world's longest suspension bridge at the time opened, linking Brooklyn and Staten Island across the Narrows.
detail: Designed by Othmar Ammann, its 4,260-foot main span held the global record until 1981. The Robert Moses project displaced 7,000 Bay Ridge residents and spurred Staten Island's suburban boom. A misspelling of Verrazzano's name persisted officially until a 2018 correction.

title: Malcolm X is assassinated in Washington Heights
blurb: The Black nationalist leader was shot dead while addressing his Organization of Afro-American Unity at the Audubon Ballroom in upper Manhattan.
detail: Malcolm X had broken with the Nation of Islam and faced escalating threats before the attack. Three men were convicted, though two convictions were vacated in 2021 after a reinvestigation found withheld evidence. His autobiography, published later that year, made him a lasting icon of Black self-determination.

sources.name: National Museum of African American History and Culture

title: The Landmarks Preservation Law is signed
blurb: Mayor Robert Wagner signed the law creating the Landmarks Preservation Commission, spurred by outrage over Penn Station's demolition.
detail: The law gave the city power to designate and protect buildings, interiors and historic districts. Grand Central Terminal's later survival, upheld by the Supreme Court in 1978, became the movement's landmark victory. The commission has since designated tens of thousands of protected properties.

title: Immigration reform reopens the golden door
blurb: President Johnson signed the Hart-Celler Immigration and Nationality Act at the Statue of Liberty, ending national-origin quotas that had favored Europe.
detail: The law prioritized family reunification and skills, opening the door to large-scale migration from Asia, the Caribbean and Latin America. Over the following decades it transformed New York into a truly global city with immigrant populations from every continent. Neighborhoods like Flushing, Jackson Heights and Sunset Park were remade by the new arrivals.

sources.name: U.S. State Department, Office of the Historian

title: The Great Northeast Blackout
blurb: A cascading power failure plunged New York City and much of the Northeast into darkness, stranding 800,000 riders in the subways.
detail: A faulty relay in Ontario triggered the failure that darkened 80,000 square miles and left some 30 million people without power. New Yorkers were widely praised for calm, orderly behavior through the night. The event exposed the fragility of the interconnected grid and led to new reliability standards.

title: The Ocean Hill-Brownsville teachers' strike
blurb: A battle over community control of schools in a Black Brooklyn district triggered citywide teacher strikes that shut schools for weeks.
detail: The experimental district's board dismissed mostly white, largely Jewish teachers, and the union led three strikes affecting more than a million students. The conflict exposed raw tensions between Black self-determination and labor rights. Its bitter legacy shaped New York school governance and race relations for years.

title: Shirley Chisholm is elected to Congress
blurb: The Brooklyn educator became the first Black woman elected to the U.S. Congress, representing Bedford-Stuyvesant.
detail: Running under the slogan 'Unbought and Unbossed,' Chisholm won the newly drawn 12th District seat. In 1972 she became the first Black candidate to seek a major party's presidential nomination. She served seven terms and was a founding member of the Congressional Black Caucus.

title: The Stonewall uprising ignites gay liberation
blurb: Patrons fought back against a police raid at the Stonewall Inn in Greenwich Village, sparking days of protest that launched the modern LGBTQ movement.
detail: The Mafia-run bar was one of the few places gay, lesbian and transgender New Yorkers could gather openly. Figures like Marsha P. Johnson and Sylvia Rivera became central to the resistance. The first Pride marches a year later commemorated the uprising, and the site became a national monument in 2016.

title: The World Trade Center's north tower tops out
blurb: Steelworkers set the final beam atop One World Trade Center, briefly making it the tallest building in the world.
detail: Designed by Minoru Yamasaki for the Port Authority, the Twin Towers rose 110 stories on a superblock in Lower Manhattan. The project displaced the electronics shops of Radio Row and used innovative tube-frame engineering. The complex was formally dedicated in April 1973.

title: Hip-hop is born in the Bronx
blurb: DJ Kool Herc spun back-to-back breakbeats at a rec-room party at 1520 Sedgwick Ave., an event widely credited as the birth of hip-hop.
detail: Herc's 'merry-go-round' technique extended the danceable breaks of records, giving rise to breakdancing and MCing. From the West Bronx the culture spread through DJs like Afrika Bambaataa and Grandmaster Flash. Within a decade it grew from block parties into a global cultural and commercial force.

title: 'Ford to City: Drop Dead'
blurb: The Daily News ran its famous headline after President Ford said he would veto a federal bailout of a near-bankrupt New York City.
detail: The city, unable to sell its debt, teetered on default after years of borrowing to cover deficits. The state created the Municipal Assistance Corporation and the Emergency Financial Control Board to seize the city's finances. Ford relented in December, and the headline is often credited with costing him New York in 1976.

title: Studio 54 opens
blurb: The Midtown discotheque opened and instantly became the epicenter of celebrity nightlife and disco excess.
detail: Founders Steve Rubell and Ian Schrager ran the former opera house and TV studio with a famously selective door. Andy Warhol, Bianca Jagger and Halston made it a global symbol of the era's hedonism. A 1980 tax-evasion conviction sent the owners to prison and ended the club's original run.

sources.name: Museum of the City of New York

title: The blackout that broke the city's spirit
blurb: Lightning strikes knocked out power for some 25 hours, unleashing widespread looting and arson across the crime-plagued, cash-strapped city.
detail: Unlike the orderly 1965 blackout, this one saw more than 1,600 stores damaged and roughly 3,800 people arrested. Bushwick, Brooklyn and other struggling neighborhoods burned amid the chaos. The night crystallized the sense of a city in deep crisis during its 'Bronx is burning' summer.

sources.name: Museum of the City of New York

title: The Son of Sam is captured
blurb: Police arrested David Berkowitz, ending a serial-killing spree that terrorized the city for more than a year.
detail: Berkowitz shot young couples in cars, killing six and wounding others while taunting police with letters signed 'Son of Sam.' A parking ticket near a crime scene ultimately led to his arrest in Yonkers. His crimes deepened the fear that gripped New York during the summer of 1977.

title: Ed Koch is elected mayor
blurb: The brash congressman won the mayoralty, beginning three terms that spanned the city's recovery from fiscal collapse.
detail: Koch, known for greeting New Yorkers with 'How'm I doin'?', restored the city's access to credit markets and balanced its budget. His tenure also saw racial tensions, a corruption scandal among allies and the early AIDS crisis. He served 12 years before losing to David Dinkins in the 1989 Democratic primary.

sources.name: NYC Mayors, City of New York

title: John Lennon is murdered outside the Dakota
blurb: The former Beatle was shot and killed by Mark David Chapman at the entrance to his Upper West Side apartment building.
detail: Lennon, 40, had just returned home from a recording session with Yoko Ono when he was gunned down. Tens of thousands gathered in nearby Central Park to mourn, and the city later dedicated the Strawberry Fields memorial. Chapman, who had earlier gotten Lennon's autograph that day, was sentenced to 20 years to life.

title: The AIDS epidemic surfaces in New York
blurb: The New York Times reported a 'rare cancer seen in 41 homosexuals,' one of the first public alerts to what became the AIDS crisis.
detail: The article followed the CDC's June report of unusual pneumonia cases in gay men and described Kaposi's sarcoma clusters in New York and California. The city became an epicenter of the epidemic, which killed tens of thousands of New Yorkers. Groups like ACT UP, founded in 1987, mobilized to demand treatment and research.

title: Bernhard Goetz shoots four on the subway
blurb: A white man shot four young Black men he said were trying to rob him on a downtown IRT train, becoming a lightning rod in the fearful city.
detail: Goetz, dubbed the 'subway vigilante,' surrendered days later and was acquitted of attempted murder in 1987, convicted only of illegal gun possession. The case exposed deep anxieties over crime, race and self-defense at the height of the subway's decline. One victim was left paralyzed and later won a large civil judgment.

sources.name: Museum of the City of New York

title: The crack epidemic engulfs the city
blurb: Cheap, smokable crack cocaine flooded New York neighborhoods, driving a surge in addiction and violent crime.
detail: First appearing around 1985, crack devastated communities in Harlem, the South Bronx and central Brooklyn. The drug trade fueled turf wars that pushed the city's murder count toward a 1990 peak of more than 2,200. The crisis also spurred harsh sentencing laws whose effects fell heavily on Black and Latino New Yorkers.

title: The Tompkins Square Park riot
blurb: A clash between police and protesters over a curfew in the East Village park erupted into a night of violence and later findings of police brutality.
detail: The park had become a flashpoint over homelessness, gentrification and squatting on the Lower East Side. Officers charged crowds, and civilian complaints and video led to investigations of police misconduct. The confrontation became a symbol of the era's battles over who the changing city belonged to.

sources.name: Museum of the City of New York

title: The Central Park jogger case
blurb: A young woman was raped and nearly beaten to death in Central Park, and five Black and Latino teenagers were wrongfully convicted of the attack.
detail: Amid a media frenzy and calls for harsh punishment, the 'Central Park Five' were coerced into confessions and imprisoned. In 2002 a serial rapist confessed and DNA evidence confirmed his guilt, and the convictions were vacated. The men, later known as the Exonerated Five, won a $41 million settlement from the city.

title: David Dinkins is elected New York's first Black mayor
blurb: The Manhattan borough president defeated Rudolph Giuliani to become the city's first African American mayor.
detail: Dinkins had ousted incumbent Ed Koch in the Democratic primary and promised to heal a city he called a 'gorgeous mosaic.' His single term was marked by budget strains, the Crown Heights unrest and the start of a crime decline he began funding through the Safe Streets program. He narrowly lost a 1993 rematch to Giuliani.

title: Murders peak amid the crack era
blurb: New York City recorded a record 2,245 murders in 1990, its highest annual total, during the height of the crack epidemic.
detail: The toll capped years of rising violence tied to the crack trade and made public safety the city's dominant political issue. Crime then began a long, steep decline over the following decades. By the 2010s annual murders had fallen below 300.

title: The Crown Heights unrest
blurb: Three days of violence erupted in the Brooklyn neighborhood after a car in a Hasidic motorcade struck and killed a Black child, Gavin Cato.
detail: Hours later a rabbinical student, Yankel Rosenbaum, was fatally stabbed amid the turmoil that pitted Black and Orthodox Jewish residents against each other. The clashes exposed long-simmering tensions and drew criticism of the Dinkins administration's response. A state report later faulted the police handling of the crisis.

title: Terrorists bomb the World Trade Center
blurb: A truck bomb exploded in the parking garage beneath the Twin Towers, killing six people and injuring more than a thousand.
detail: Islamic extremists led by Ramzi Yousef sought to topple one tower into the other but the structure held. Thousands were evacuated through smoke-filled stairwells in the largest such rescue in city history to that point. The attack was a grim prelude to the far deadlier assault on the same site in 2001.

title: Rudolph Giuliani is elected mayor
blurb: The former federal prosecutor narrowly defeated David Dinkins, ushering in a Republican administration focused on crime and quality of life.
detail: Giuliani campaigned on public safety after years of soaring crime and racial strife. His administration embraced 'broken windows' policing and the CompStat data system, presiding over one of the sharpest crime declines in modern American history. His tenure also drew sustained criticism over police abuses and race relations.

title: CompStat and the great crime decline
blurb: The NYPD under Commissioner William Bratton launched CompStat, a data-driven policing system credited with helping drive crime down for years.
detail: CompStat mapped crime in near real time and held precinct commanders accountable at intense weekly meetings. Paired with 'broken windows' tactics, it accompanied a plunge in murders from more than 2,200 in 1990 to under 700 by decade's end. Scholars still debate how much of the drop was due to policing versus broader national trends.

title: The Yankees' dynasty begins
blurb: The New York Yankees won the World Series over the Atlanta Braves, their first title since 1978 and the start of a late-1990s dynasty.
detail: Managed by Joe Torre, a young core including Derek Jeter and Mariano Rivera rallied from a 2-0 series deficit. The team went on to win championships in 1998, 1999 and 2000, capping the last with a Subway Series over the Mets. The run restored the franchise's aura at the close of the century.

title: Times Square is reborn
blurb: Disney's The Lion King opened at the restored New Amsterdam Theatre, symbolizing the transformation of a once-seedy Times Square into a family tourist mecca.
detail: A public-private redevelopment effort cleared out peep shows and adult theaters along 42nd Street. Disney's Broadway investment anchored a wave of new hotels, retail and megastores through the late 1990s. Critics lamented the loss of grit, while boosters hailed a safer, booming crossroads of the world.

sources.name: Museum of the City of New York

title: The police killing of Amadou Diallo
blurb: Four plainclothes officers fired 41 shots at an unarmed West African immigrant in the Bronx, killing him and igniting mass protests.
detail: Diallo, 23, was reaching for his wallet when officers of the NYPD's Street Crime Unit opened fire in his Soundview doorway. The officers were acquitted of all charges in 2000, sparking days of civil disobedience and hundreds of arrests. The case became a defining symbol of tensions over aggressive policing under Giuliani.

sources.name: New York State Unified Court System
